text: Well, stick me in a dress and call me
Shirley because this story seems to have
more twists and turns than an Agatha
Christie novel. Just when you thought it
was all over, along comes a last minute
play to completely change the game. It
seemed like only yesterday I was
reporting on the sale of Warner Brothers
to Netflix after an intense bidden war
with Paramount in a hundred billion
dollar mega deal that seemed to signal a
shift in the balance of power in
Hollywoods. the beginning of the end for
both the studio system and the
theatrical release model as all the
major players were slowly being gobbled
up and absorbed into the streaming
giants. But then Paramount took a look
at the situation and decided,
>> "Maybe so, sir,
but not today."
>> And promptly upped their bid to a
frankly ludicrous $111 billion. At which
point, Netflix decided, "Yeah, [ __ ]
that, bro. We're out." And just like
that, Paramount had pulled victory from
the jaws of defeat, seeing off Netflix
in an exercise of raw financial power.
But what the hell does this even mean
for the future of film making,
theatrical releases, and the massive
slate of IPs and divisions under the
Warner Brothers umbrella? Because let's
not mince words here. Paramount now
control a pretty massive slice of the
media landscape. Not just entertainment
IPs, movies, and TV shows, but news
networks as well. What are they going to
do with all this power and influence?
Well, to answer that question, you
should probably consider the man at the
top of this particular pyramid, David
Ellison, the billionaire owner of
Paramount Sky Dance and the son of Larry
Ellison, the world's third richest man
with a net worth of almost $250 billion.
[ __ ] Ellison started in the industry by
founding Sky Dance Media back in 2006
with nothing more than a little elbow
grease, some can do attitude, and yes, a
massive infusion of money from his
dad's. What can I say, man? Having the
world's third richest man as your father
probably has a few perks. Anyway, fast
forward a couple of decades, and David
was keen to expand his business empire,
and one quick and efficient way to do
that was to buy out the struggling
Paramount Pictures, which was the studio
equivalent of a fixer upper. It had lots
of valuable IPs and networks under its
control, but years of bad decisions and
corporate restructures meant that it no
longer had the resources and confidence
to exploit them. That's great and all,
but you might think that the FCC would
have a thing or two to say about such a
huge merger of corporations, especially
when it comes to news channels like CBS
on the line. Not to worry, though,
because as it turns out, David is good
pals with the man in the White House.
And I kind of like to imagine the
conversation played out something like
this.
What do you want?
>> Hey, Don, any chance you could lean on
the FCC and get them to approve that Sky
Dance merger for me? I sure would
appreciate it, buddy.
>> I don't know about that, Dave. People
might have questions.
>> Well, if you could make this happen,
I'll fire everyone at CBS and replace
them with people who only say nice
things about you.
>> Consider it done.
>> Cheers, bro. And so, approval was
granted and the deal went ahead.
Paramount became Paramount Sky Dance.
And David set his sights on his next

with a shitload of potential and a ton

funnily enough, another news network in
need of uh reconfiguring.
>> Ohoy there,
>> Don. It's me again. Listen, you know how

Brothers this time.
>> I don't know if I can do this a second

>> they have CNN.
>> We've got a deal.
>> The upshot of all this is that I

>> you'll be seeing a lot of changes around
here.
>> But what does it mean for the

what we're really interested in here?
Well, based on Ellison's previous work
as a producer for Sky Dance, it's not
difficult to see where his creative
sensibilities lie. He's very much an
action guy with a heavy leaning towards
maledriven properties with traditional
heroes. And how can I put this? I don't
think he's exactly a big fan of the
message.
In fact, considering Paramount Plus is
rapidly turning into the Taylor Sheridan
network, I think it's fair to say this
guy sees middle America for what it is,
a potentially huge market that's been
badly neglected in recent years, mostly
by people who absolutely hate them.
Imagine for a moment if there was a
network that catered to their interests.
Now, as you can probably surmise, the
prospect of a multi-billionaire with
conservative leanings merging two of the
biggest studios in Hollywood together
under his personal control went down
about as well as expected with the usual
crowds.
Oh dear. It's a bit scary when you don't
have an absolute strangle hold on modern
culture and have to compete with other
creatives that don't share your
particular worldview, isn't it? Pass me
the world's smallest violin, will you? I

and have the entire Secret Hideout team

[ __ ] it. You can just buy out their

you're short on money. But whatever you
do, just get those useless retards out
of there. But what about Warner Brothers
other IPs like Lord of the Rings, Game

DCU? Well, I'd be lying if I said I was
disappointed they weren't going to fall
under Netflix's control. I mean, Jesus,
could you even begin to imagine what a
Netflix adaptation of Lord of the Rings
would look like. We may just have dodged
the biggest bullet in entertainment
history. Wouldn't get too comfortable in
that Warner Brothers office if I were
you James.
That being said, I'm not going to
pretend that this is an ideal outcome by
any stretch. And I don't particularly
love the idea of independent studios
merging together into homogeneous mega
corporations because let's be honest,
it's bad for competition and bad for
creativity. But on the other hand, if
ever there was a definitive signal that
the age of identity politics and the
message has come to an end in Hollywood,
I think this just might be it. The great
experiment that's decimated pop culture
for the past decade is well and truly
over. Good [ __ ] riddance. The only
question now is when can the age of
rebuilding begin?
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
